proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The core activities (a city‑wide walking day, a tracking app, and prize incentives) are technically possible and similar events have been run elsewhere. However, the proposal relies on several unverified prerequisites – 5 % population participation, government authorization, and a custom app that accurately converts steps to GHG savings – without a clear implementation plan or risk mitigation, creating moderate uncertainty about successful execution.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days, bike‑to‑work programs, and mobile‑app tracking of personal emissions are already established worldwide. The proposal recombines existing ideas rather than introducing a fundamentally new mechanism or technology.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact estimates (e.g., 66 tCO₂e saved in a single day, 105 k tCO₂e / yr long‑term) are derived from highly simplified assumptions (fixed emission factor of 0.1 kg CO₂ / km, constant 5 % participation, linear extrapolation to national and global scales). Empirical evidence suggests actual emissions reductions from a one‑day event would be an order of magnitude lower, so the projected climate benefit is likely overstated.

presentation.rationale: The document contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, missing figures (referenced images not provided), and calculations that are not fully sourced. While the overall concept is understandable, the lack of clear, polished presentation hampers credibility and readability.

A1.perspective_summary: From a scientific and systems perspective, the "LET'S WALK!" event is technically feasible but hinges on optimistic participation rates and untested data‑collection methods. Its novelty is limited, and the claimed emissions reductions rely on simplistic assumptions that introduce substantial uncertainty. The proposal’s presentation is fragmented, reducing its persuasive power.
key_concerns: Assumption of 5 % city‑wide participation without evidence of recruitment strategy.
Accuracy of the app’s conversion of steps/bike trips to GHG savings; algorithm validation is missing.
Reliance on government and corporate sponsorships that are not quantified or secured.
Simplified emission factors and linear scaling to national/global levels may overstate impact.
Absence of a detailed risk‑mitigation or contingency plan for low uptake or technical failures.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires multiple government authorizations, coordination with transport agencies, health services, NGOs and private sponsors, which are institutionally available in Buenos Aires. However, the proposal provides no concrete funding plan, legal basis for the smartphone‑app data collection, or enforcement mechanisms, creating uncertainty about implementation.

novelty.rationale: One‑day car‑free or walking events and app‑based tracking have been used in many cities (e.g., Earth Hour, car‑free days). The concept is therefore not novel; the only marginally new element is the integration of a prize‑incentive scheme tied to GHG‑saving calculations.

impact.rationale: The proposal quantifies short‑term and long‑term CO₂e savings, but the estimates rely on optimistic participation rates and unverified conversion factors. Governance impact (policy change, habit formation) could be moderate if the event is repeated, yet the single‑day design limits lasting systemic change.

presentation.rationale: The document contains extensive calculations and references but suffers from poor organization, typographical errors, and unclear sections (e.g., vague timelines, mixed language). This hampers quick policy assessment.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the "LET'S WALK!" event leverages existing city infrastructure and potential NGO and corporate partners, yet it lacks a clear regulatory framework, funding strategy, and data‑privacy safeguards. While the ambition to raise climate awareness is commendable, the proposal’s operational details are insufficient for reliable policy implementation.
key_concerns: Absence of a legal basis for collecting and sharing location/step data; compliance with Argentina's data‑protection law (Ley de Protección de Datos Personales).
Unclear financing: no budget breakdown, reliance on unspecified sponsor contributions and government support.
No formal inter‑agency agreement outlined between the city transport authority, health services, and the organizing team.
Reliance on a single‑day event limits habit formation; sustainability of impact beyond the event is not addressed.
Equity considerations: participants must be able to walk or bike; the plan does not address accessibility for people with disabilities or those living far from work.
Verification of reported GHG savings is not described; independent audit mechanisms are needed.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on multiple stakeholders (city government, NGOs, sponsors, security forces, health services) and on developing or licensing a smartphone app to track walking/biking. Securing funding for prizes, media campaigns, and data‑privacy compliance adds complexity. While Buenos Aires already has a bike‑share system that can be leveraged, achieving the assumed 5 % participation and coordinating a city‑wide one‑day effort present moderate operational risk, placing feasibility at an average level.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days are widely practiced worldwide. Adding an app that records steps and rewards the largest GHG‑savings introduces a modest twist, but the core concept is not new. Hence the proposal scores low on novelty.

impact.rationale: The authors estimate ~66 tCO₂e saved on the event day and extrapolate to ~39 MtCO₂e / yr globally, which appears optimistic for a single‑day activity. Direct emissions reductions are modest, though the awareness and habit‑forming potential could generate incremental long‑term benefits. Therefore the impact is assessed as moderate.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains numerous grammatical errors, inconsistent formatting, and some unclear sections. While it provides quantitative assumptions and visual assets, the overall readability and logical flow are weak, resulting in a below‑average presentation score.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, the "Let's Walk" event is feasible with coordinated government support, sponsor funding, and a reliable app, but it faces moderate risk due to stakeholder alignment and participant recruitment. The idea is not particularly novel, and its direct emissions impact is limited, though it could foster longer‑term behavioral change. Presentation quality hampers clear communication of the plan.
key_concerns: Securing government authorization and coordination with city transport authorities.
Funding and technical development of the tracking app, including data‑privacy compliance.
Achieving the assumed 5 % population participation without strong pre‑event outreach.
Reliability of the emissions‑saving calculations and potential over‑estimation.
Sustaining momentum after a single‑day event to create lasting habit change.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on a smartphone app, government permits, security forces, health staff and prize sponsors. While these resources exist in Buenos Aires, the proposal does not address practical barriers for many residents: people with disabilities, those who lack safe sidewalks, or who cannot afford a smartphone. Coordination of multiple actors (government, NGOs, companies) is plausible but unproven, making the overall feasibility moderate.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑to‑work or car‑free days have been run in many cities, so the core idea is not new. The addition of a tracking app that converts steps into GHG‑savings and a prize competition adds a modest layer of novelty, but the concept remains largely incremental.

impact.rationale: The proposal projects 66 tCO₂e saved in a single day and larger long‑term savings, but these numbers rely on a 5 % participation assumption and on participants fully replacing car trips with walking or biking. From a community view, health benefits for participants are clear, yet many residents (elderly, disabled, low‑income workers with long commutes) cannot realistically join, limiting equitable impact. The long‑term habit change is speculative.

presentation.rationale: The document contains detailed calculations and references, but it is poorly organized, includes many typographical errors, and refers to figures that are not shown. This hampers comprehension for local stakeholders and community groups who need clear, concise information.

A4.perspective_summary: From the affected‑community standpoint, LET'S WALK! offers a visible, health‑promoting event that could raise climate awareness, but its success hinges on inclusive design, safe walking infrastructure, and equitable access to the required technology. Without addressing these ground‑level issues, participation may be limited to a relatively privileged segment, reducing the promised environmental and social benefits.
key_concerns: Accessibility for people with mobility impairments or those living beyond walkable distances.
Safety of walking routes and adequacy of bike lanes, especially in high‑traffic neighborhoods.
Digital divide: reliance on a smartphone app excludes residents without smartphones or data plans.
Data privacy and consent for tracking individual movements.
Realistic participation rates: 5 % of the city may be optimistic without strong outreach.
Weather and seasonal factors that could deter walking on the chosen day.
Potential unintended burden on emergency services and security personnel during the event.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on voluntary participation, a smartphone app, and coordination with government, NGOs, and sponsors. While these partners exist, the proposal lacks concrete plans for data privacy, equitable access to the app, and realistic budgeting for security and health personnel, making implementation plausible but uncertain.

novelty.rationale: A city‑wide walking day with gamified emissions tracking is a modest twist on existing bike‑to‑work and Earth Hour campaigns. The concept is not fundamentally new, though the combination of walking incentives, home‑office promotion, and big‑data collection adds some originality.

impact.rationale: Projected emissions savings (≈66 tCO₂e per day, 0.38 % of transport sector emissions globally) rely on a 5 % participation rate and assume all participants have the means to walk or bike. Benefits accrue mainly to participants and sponsors, while costs (security, health services, data collection) fall on public resources. Exclusion of low‑income residents without smartphones or safe walking routes limits distributive justice.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains many typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and a scattered narrative that hampers clear understanding. Numerical assumptions are presented without clear sources, and the structure makes it difficult to assess feasibility and equity.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity standpoint, the LET'S WALK! event promises modest climate benefits but risks reinforcing existing inequalities: it privileges participants who own smartphones, can safely walk or bike, and have flexible work arrangements, while shifting public costs (security, health, data infrastructure) onto taxpayers. The reliance on corporate sponsors for data and prizes raises hidden value conflicts between climate goals and profit motives.
key_concerns: Potential exclusion of low‑income or mobility‑limited residents who cannot walk, bike, or access the tracking app.
Data privacy and surveillance risks associated with a government‑partnered app that collects location and travel data.
Unequal distribution of costs: public funds for security, health personnel, and media versus private benefits for sponsors.
The proposal’s emphasis on individual behavior change may divert attention from needed systemic transport and urban planning reforms.

feasibility.rationale: The event hinges on coordinated action among government, NGOs, sponsors, and a custom smartphone app. Achieving 5 % city‑wide participation (≈145 k people) is ambitious but comparable to other large‑scale awareness days. The need for app development, security, and media spend introduces moderate risk, so feasibility is moderate.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and car‑free events already exist worldwide. Using an app to log steps and award prizes adds an incremental twist, but the core idea is not novel.

impact.rationale: Short‑term CO₂e savings (~66 t day⁻¹) are modest relative to city emissions. Long‑term benefits (10 % rise in recycling, 5 % solar law adoption, 2 % electric‑bus lines) are speculative and rely on policy changes not directly caused by the event. Thus, systemic climate impact is limited.

presentation.rationale: The proposal provides quantitative estimates and visual references, which is valuable. However, it suffers from disorganized structure, typographical errors, and unclear sections that hinder quick comprehension.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability viewpoint, LET'S WALK! is a well‑intentioned awareness campaign that could modestly reduce emissions on the event day and potentially seed healthier commuting habits. Yet the novelty is low, the projected systemic impacts are largely conjectural, and the logistical complexity makes the overall feasibility moderate. The presentation conveys the idea but needs clearer organization.
key_concerns: Evidence that a single‑day event translates into durable behavior change is lacking.
Reliance on a proprietary app for accurate distance and emissions accounting introduces measurement uncertainty.
Potential rebound effects (e.g., increased car use on other days) are not addressed.
Equity concerns: low‑income residents may lack safe walking routes or access to bikes.
Long‑term policy outcomes (recycling increase, solar law, electric buses) depend on separate legislative actions not guaranteed by the event.

feasibility.rationale: All experts agree the event is technically possible using existing city infrastructure, bike‑share, and a tracking app, but it hinges on securing government authorizations, funding, and achieving the assumed 5 % participation, which remains uncertain.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑to‑work/car‑free days and emissions‑tracking apps are already common; the proposal adds only a modest gamified prize element, so it offers little new methodology or technology.

impact.rationale: Calculated CO₂ savings rely on optimistic participation and conversion factors; direct emissions reduction is modest, yet the event could foster medium‑term habit change, giving it a moderate overall impact.

presentation.rationale: The document contains many typographical errors, missing figures, and a disorganized structure, which hampers readability and credibility despite the inclusion of quantitative estimates.

final.synthesis: The experts converge on a moderate feasibility, low novelty, moderate impact, and weak presentation. The event leverages existing Buenos Aires assets (bike‑share, NGOs, media) and could raise climate awareness, but its success depends on realistic recruitment, robust data‑privacy safeguards, equitable access, and clear funding and risk‑mitigation plans. The novelty is limited to a gamified incentive layer, and the projected emissions reductions are likely overstated. Presentation quality needs substantial improvement to persuade stakeholders.
final.uncertainty: Key uncertainties remain around actual participation rates, the accuracy and validation of the app’s emissions‑saving algorithm, compliance with Argentina’s data‑protection law, and whether a single‑day event can generate durable behavioral change and systemic policy outcomes.
